content.text: as their only argument. When you need to pass additional data to a handler — like the ID of the item being deleted or the index of a tab being clicked — you need a pattern that bridges JSX's one-argument constraint with your handler's need for more context.

attrs.alt: Three ways to pass arguments to event handlers: arrow wrapper, data- attribute, and curried factory
attrs.title: Passing arguments to handlers

content.text: Why You Can't Just Call the Handler
React stores the function you pass to an event prop and calls it with the event object when the event fires. You can't change that calling convention — the handler gets
. The patterns below all work around this constraint without calling the handler during render.
Wrap the call in an arrow function. The arrow is what React stores; it closes over your data and calls the real handler with it when the event fires.

Each arrow function is a new object created each render. For a list of 50 items this means 50 new functions per render. That is almost always fine — React renders are fast and function allocation is cheap. Only optimize if profiling shows it is a bottleneck.
If you also need the event object (to call
or read its properties), include it as a parameter in the arrow and forward it.
attribute on the element and read it from
in a single shared handler. This avoids creating a new function for every list item.
attributes only work on HTML elements. They serialize to strings, so you must parse numeric IDs back with
— if the button has a child element (like an icon),
might point to the icon, not the button.
A curried function takes the argument first and returns the actual handler. You call the factory in JSX — which is fine because it returns a function (not

The factory still creates a new function per render (one per item). Its advantage over the inline arrow is readability when the inner function is more than a one-liner, and the factory itself is independently testable.
When state is lifted to a parent, the child receives callbacks as props. The same arrow-wrapper pattern applies — close over the item-specific data and call the prop callback with it.
When a handler is passed to a
dependency, the new-function-per-render issue becomes real. Wrap the per-item arrow in
with the ID as a dependency to stabilize it.
useCallback on per-item handlers inside a map is rarely the right optimization. Profile first. If the list is small or renders infrequently, the arrow wrapper with no memoization is fine.
A Full Worked Example: Action Buttons on a Table
This employee table demonstrates three distinct actions per row — view, edit, delete — each receiving the row's ID through arrow wrappers.

The Delete button passes the event to stop propagation — if it didn't, clicking Delete would also trigger the row's view action. This is a common pattern when a child element needs to intercept an event that would otherwise bubble to a parent.
Mistake 2: Forgetting to parse data- values
Mistake 3: Using event.target instead of event.currentTarget with data-
When a handler needs more than one piece of data — the item ID plus a new value, or an index and a field name — pass them all through the arrow wrapper.
pattern with a computed property key (
) is a clean way to unify all field updates into one handler while keeping each input's intent clear through the first argument.
Passing Arguments in Context: Form with Dynamic Rows
Dynamic forms — where rows can be added and removed, and any cell can be edited — combine several argument-passing patterns at once.
This example shows three argument-passing needs side-by-side:
with none. Each is handled by an arrow wrapper in JSX that closes over the loop variable.

When you build your own components, the callback props you expose can accept any arguments — not just a SyntheticEvent. Design them around the data the consumer needs, not around the underlying DOM event.
Anti-pattern: Hardcoding Arguments Inside the Component
Avoid defining a separate named handler for every list item. This scales with the number of items and produces repetitive, hard-to-maintain code.
Wrap handlers in an arrow to pass arguments:
Pass the event too when needed:
attributes for a shared handler on HTML elements; parse the string value and read from
Curried factories return a function (safe to call in JSX) and keep the handler body clean.
Arrow wrappers create a new function per item per render — this is rarely a problem; only add memoization after profiling.
